Slowly pour in the chicken broth while stirring, scraping the bottom of the pot with the spoon to loosen any stuck on flour or vegetables.

Add the milk, salt, and black pepper. Bring to a simmer. Reduce the heat just high enough to maintain a low simmer, low to medium-low. (Try to keep it from boiling hard.) Partially cover and continue simmering until the potatoes are very tender, about 40 minutes. For a thicker and less chunky soup, mash the potatoes with the back of a spoon or a potato masher to your desired consistency. Stir in the cream and sour cream. Taste and add more salt and pepper, if desired. Serve immediately topped with sour cream, cheese, chives, and the cooked bacon.
notes: Don't toss the bacon drippings! After you cook the bacon, be sure to save the drippings to cook the onion and garlic in. It adds a lot of great flavor to the soup. If you don't want to use bacon in the soup, skip this step and melt 4 tablespoons of butter in the pot before adding the veggies.
Create a smooth texture. If you prefer a smooth soup texture rather than chunky, use an immersion blender to blend the soup to your desired consistency once it's cooked.

Make it gluten-free. Omit the flour and instead whisk in one tablespoon of cornstarch into the milk. Then add the milk to the recipe as outlined. Or, you can substitute your favorite brand of 1-to-1 gluten-free flour. Either option will make the soup just as thick!

Add extra toppings. Load it up! Try adding seasonings like fresh thyme, red chili flakes, and extra black pepper, or add a dash of hot sauce. Use a mix of shredded cheeses like cheddar and smoked Gouda, or try shredded goat cheese. Or stick to the classics- sour cream, chives or green onions, and cheddar cheese!

Options variations. Sneak in some extra veggies by adding chopped cabbage, spinach, broccoli, cauliflower, carrots, corn, peas, or leeks when you add the potatoes to the pot. Additionally, you can add a blend of seasonings, such as Cajun or Italian seasoning mix, for different flavors.
title: Potato Soup

author: Amanda Cooks & Styles
description: Super simple homemade breakfast potato recipe made with fresh potatoes, herbs and seasonings. Serve with fresh cooked eggs, toast, in a breakfast burrito and more.
ingredients: 1 tablespoon Chopped Parsley, fresh, or 1/2 teaspoon dried
instructions: Cut all potatoes into small 1/2-inch cubes.
Heat skillet to medium high heat and add butter and oil.

Add chopped potatoes to the hot skillet and toss around to fully coat in butter/oil combination. Sprinkle potatoes with seasonings except for the parsley. Cover to let potatoes lightly steam for about 3-4 minutes.
Remove lid and stir potatoes. Continue to cook potatoes with lid off, turning potatoes around with spatula to ensure an even cook on all sides.
When potatoes are done they will have a light crisp on the outside, soft on the inside.
Sprinkle potatoes with fresh parsley and serve.
title: Easy Breakfast Potatoes

author: Robert Carr
description: A reverse-seared thick-cut Wagyu-style ribeye using a measured salt dry brine, low oven cook, final dry pat, and hard stainless steel pan sear with an optional brief garlic-thyme butter baste.
ingredients: 1 thick-cut ribeye steak, about 1 pound and at least 1 1/2 inches thick
3.4 grams kosher salt, about 0.75% of the steak weight
1/2 teaspoon black pepper, freshly ground, added after searing or lightly before oven step
1 to 2 teaspoons avocado oil, or another neutral high-heat oil
1 pinch flaky salt, optional, for finishing
instructions: Thaw steak fully in the refrigerator if frozen.
Weigh the steak in grams if possible.
Calculate kosher salt as 0.75% of the steak weight. Use the formula: steak weight in grams multiplied by 0.0075 equals salt in grams.
For a 1 pound steak, use about 3.4 grams kosher salt total for the top, bottom, edges, and fat cap.
Remove steak from packaging and pat very dry on all sides with paper towels.
Season steak evenly on all sides with the measured kosher salt only.
Place a wire rack over a baking sheet or plate, then place the steak on the wire rack uncovered.
Refrigerate uncovered for 4 to 8 hours, or up to 12 hours if using the conservative 0.75% salt amount. This dry-brines the steak and dries the surface for a better crust.
Remove steak from the refrigerator 30 to 45 minutes before cooking.
Pat steak dry again if any surface moisture appears.
Preheat oven to 225°F.
Place steak on a wire rack over a baking sheet and cook in the oven until the center reaches 115°F to 118°F for classic medium-rare, or 118°F to 120°F for medium-rare leaning medium.

Begin checking the internal temperature around 20 minutes using an instant-read thermometer inserted into the center from the side. Continue checking every 5 minutes, then every 2 to 3 minutes once close to the target temperature.
Remove steak from oven and rest for 5 to 10 minutes on a wire rack or cutting board while preheating the stainless steel pan.
Preheat a stainless steel pan on induction level 6.5 to 7 until a few drops of water bead and glide across the pan.
Just before searing, pat the steak very dry on all sides again.
Wipe out any water from the pan, add avocado oil, and heat until shimmering.
If the steak has a pronounced fat cap, hold it with tongs and render the fat cap for 30 to 60 seconds first.
Place steak in the pan and sear the broad sides for 45 to 75 seconds per side, pressing gently to ensure good pan contact.
Sear any remaining edges using tongs until browned.
Reduce heat slightly, add butter, smashed garlic, and thyme if using, then baste briefly for 20 to 30 seconds so the butter flavors the crust without overwhelming the beef.
Remove steak from pan and rest for 5 minutes on a wire rack or cutting board.
Slice against the grain.
Finish with flaky salt and black pepper if desired.
notes: Best target for this steak is medium-rare leaning medium, around 132°F to 137°F final internal temperature.
This method works best with a steak at least 1 1/2 inches thick, and even better around 1 3/4 to 2 inches thick.
Doneness targets: for medium-rare, pull from the oven at 115°F to 118°F and finish around 130°F to 135°F after searing and resting.
Doneness targets: for medium-rare leaning medium, pull from the oven at 118°F to 120°F and finish around 132°F to 137°F after searing and resting.
Doneness targets: for medium, pull from the oven at 122°F to 125°F and finish around 138°F to 145°F after searing and resting.
For a fattier Wagyu-style ribeye, avoid rare because the fat may stay waxy instead of softening and rendering.
Use 0.75% salt by steak weight for a conservative dry brine. Formula: steak weight in grams multiplied by 0.0075 equals salt in grams.
For this 1 pound steak, 0.75% salt is about 3.4 grams total.
A lighter 0.5% salt level would be about 2.27 grams for this steak. A normal 1.0% level would be about 4.54 grams.
If using volume instead of weight, 3.4 grams is roughly 1 heaping teaspoon Diamond Crystal kosher salt or about 2/3 to 3/4 teaspoon Morton kosher salt.
Salt before the refrigerator dry brine. Add black pepper after searing or lightly before the oven step because pepper can burn during a hard sear.
The wire rack matters because it lets air circulate around the steak. A plate traps moisture underneath and keeps the bottom wet.
The uncovered refrigerator step dries the surface. A drier surface browns faster and forms a better crust.
The final dry pat before searing is important because moisture from the oven step can soften the surface and slow browning.
The salt dry brine seasons the steak more deeply. Salt first draws out a little moisture, dissolves, then gets pulled back into the meat over time.
For best texture, dry brine this steak for 4 to 8 hours. Up to 12 hours is reasonable with the conservative 0.75% salt amount.
Avoid pushing the dry brine much past 12 hours for this steak unless using a very light salt level, because longer or heavier dry brines can make the outer texture seem slightly cured.
If short on time, even 1 hour uncovered in the fridge helps. If there is no time, salt right before cooking and pat the steak extremely dry.
Do not braise this steak. Use the oven only for the low-temperature reverse sear step, then finish in the stainless steel pan.
For the cleanest beef flavor, skip the butter baste or keep it very brief. For a steakhouse finish, use the butter, garlic, and thyme for only the last 20 to 30 seconds.
A laser thermometer is less reliable on shiny stainless steel; the water bead test and steak internal temperature are more useful.
If the pan smokes violently before the steak goes in, lower the induction level slightly.
Leftovers reheat best gently. Warm leftover steak in a 250°F oven on a wire rack over a baking sheet until the center reaches about 100°F to 110°F.
Do not reheat leftover steak back to 130°F or higher unless you are okay with it becoming more cooked than the original steak.
If desired, briefly touch up the crust after reheating by searing the cut sides in a hot pan for 10 to 20 seconds per side.
Leftover steak is also good cold or room temperature, sliced thin with flaky salt, eggs, rice, salad, or a sandwich.
personalNotes: This was really good. The steak was rich and buttery, enough that I was full halfway through. The butter baste is optional; the Wagyu-style ribeye has plenty of fat on its own. I dry-brined it for about 5 hours after thawing from frozen in the fridge. Used Central Market olive-fed Texas Wagyu.
title: Reverse-Seared Wagyu Ribeye
totalTime: 45 minutes plus 4 to 8 hours dry-brine time

author: Robert Carr
description: A Nashville hot chicken sandwich with pickle-buttermilk brined chicken thighs, a craggy fried crust, spicy hot oil, bright pickle slaw, dill pickles, comeback sauce, and toasted potato or brioche buns.

groceryIngredients.item: plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix

ingredients: 2 pounds boneless skinless chicken thighs, ideally 4 large thighs
1/2 cup pickle juice from a jar of dill pickle chips, for the brine
1/4 cup Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, for the brine
2 tablespoons plus 1/4 teaspoon smoked paprika, divided
1 tablespoon plus 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, divided
6 cups neutral frying oil, such as peanut, canola, or vegetable oil
4 sturdy potato buns or brioche buns
1 cup crinkle-cut hamburger dill pickle chips
3 cups plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix
1 tablespoon pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, for the slaw
1/4 cup mayonnaise, for the comeback sauce
1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, for the comeback sauce
1 teaspoon pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, for the comeback sauce
instructions: Trim any large loose pieces of fat from the 2 pounds boneless skinless chicken thighs.
If the chicken thighs are very thick, lightly pound them to an even 1/2 to 3/4 inch thickness.
In a large bowl, whisk together 2 cups buttermilk, 1/2 cup pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, 1/4 cup Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1 large egg, and 1 1/2 teaspoons kosher salt.
Add the chicken thighs to the buttermilk brine and toss until fully coated.
Cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or up to 24 hours.

Make the Nashville spice blend by mixing 4 tablespoons cayenne pepper, 1 tablespoon smoked paprika, 2 teaspoons chili powder, 2 teaspoons garlic powder, 2 teaspoons onion powder, 2 tablespoons light brown sugar, 1 teaspoon mustard powder, 1 teaspoon black pepper, 1 teaspoon kosher salt, and 1 teaspoon MSG if using.

Make the dredge by whisking together 2 cups all-purpose flour, 1/2 cup cornstarch, 1 teaspoon baking powder, 1 tablespoon cayenne pepper, 1 tablespoon smoked paprika, 1 teaspoon garlic powder, 1 teaspoon onion powder, 1 teaspoon black pepper, and 2 teaspoons kosher salt.
Drizzle 3 tablespoons of the buttermilk brine into the dredge and rub it in with your fingers until small clumps form.

Make the comeback sauce by stirring together 1/4 cup mayonnaise, 1 tablespoon ketchup, 1 teaspoon Dijon mustard, 1 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1 teaspoon pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder, and 1/4 teaspoon smoked paprika.
Refrigerate the comeback sauce until the sandwiches are ready to assemble.
Remove one piece of chicken from the brine and let the excess drip off.
Press the chicken firmly into the dredge on both sides.
Dip the chicken briefly back into the brine.
Press the chicken into the dredge a second time, packing the flour onto the surface to build a rough, craggy crust.
Gently shake off any loose dry flour so the coating is craggy but not dusty.
Place the breaded chicken on a wire rack and repeat with the remaining thighs.
Let the breaded chicken rest for 15 to 20 minutes while the oil heats.
Add 6 cups neutral frying oil to a Dutch oven or heavy-bottomed pot, making sure the pot is not filled more than halfway.
Heat the oil to 350°F.

Make the slaw by mixing 3 cups plain shredded cabbage and carrot coleslaw mix, 1/4 cup mayonnaise, 2 tablespoons apple cider vinegar, 1 tablespoon pickle juice from the dill pickle jar, 2 teaspoons honey, 1/2 teaspoon celery seed, 1/4 teaspoon black pepper, and a small pinch of kosher salt.
Taste the slaw and adjust with more vinegar, pickle juice, honey, or salt until it tastes bright, tangy, and lightly creamy.
Spread 2 tablespoons softened unsalted butter over the cut sides of 4 potato buns or brioche buns.
Toast the buns in a dry pan until golden brown, then set aside.
Fry the chicken in batches, keeping the oil between 325°F and 350°F, until deeply golden brown and the center reaches 175°F to 185°F, about 6 to 8 minutes depending on thickness.
Transfer the fried chicken to a wire rack.
Let the fried chicken rest for 3 to 5 minutes so the juices settle and the crust stays crisp.
Let the oil return to 350°F before frying the next batch.
Carefully ladle 1/2 cup of the hot frying oil into the Nashville spice blend.
Whisk until the spices form a glossy red hot oil.
Brush the hot oil generously over both sides of each piece of fried chicken just before assembling the sandwiches.
Spread comeback sauce on the toasted bottom buns.
Add 1 cup crinkle-cut hamburger dill pickle chips across the 4 bottom buns.
Add 1 piece of Nashville hot chicken to each sandwich.
Top each sandwich with creamy slaw.
Spread comeback sauce on the toasted top buns.
Close the sandwiches and serve immediately.
notes: For the pickle juice, buy a 16-ounce jar of crinkle-cut hamburger dill pickle chips or kosher dill pickle chips and use the liquid from that jar.
Avoid bread-and-butter pickles, sweet pickles, spicy-sweet pickles, and half-sour fermented pickles for this recipe. The pickle juice should taste salty, sour, dill-forward, and lightly garlicky.
Use a thin Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce. It should be vinegar-forward, salty, and peppery, not smoky, fruity, sweet, or extremely thick.
MSG is monosodium glutamate. It is a savory seasoning that makes the spice blend taste deeper and more restaurant-like.
Boneless skinless chicken thighs are the best choice here because they stay juicy and are more forgiving than chicken breast.
For the juiciest texture, fry thighs past the safe minimum and aim for 175°F to 185°F in the center.
The pickle juice gives the chicken extra salt, acidity, and classic fried chicken sandwich flavor.
The small clumps in the dredge help create a rougher, crunchier crust.
Resting the breaded chicken before frying helps the coating stick.
The hot oil should be mixed with the spices after frying, not before, so the sugar and cayenne do not burn.
Use 4 tablespoons cayenne in the hot oil for a hot sandwich. Use 2 tablespoons for medium heat or 5 to 6 tablespoons for very hot.
The brine is best after 8 to 12 hours, but 4 hours works and up to 24 hours is fine.
The coleslaw mix should be a plain shredded cabbage and carrot mix, not a salad kit with dressing included.
The slaw should taste tangy and fresh on its own; that acidity is what keeps the sandwich from feeling heavy.
The comeback sauce, slaw, and pickles are important because they balance the heat and richness.
Serve the sandwiches right after brushing the chicken with hot oil for the best crust.
Leftover chicken reheats best on a wire rack in a 375°F oven or air fryer until hot and crisp.
title: Nashville Hot Chicken Sandwiches
totalTime: 1 hour 5 minutes plus 4 to 24 hours brine time

author: Robert Carr
description: A double smash burger with cold 80/20 beef, lacy griddle crust, shaved onion smashed into the patties, melty American cheese, toasted potato buns, dill pickles, crisp lettuce, and homemade burger sauce.
equipment: Cast-iron griddle or large cast-iron skillet
Heavy metal spatula or burger press
ingredients: 1 1/2 pounds cold 80/20 ground beef, preferably freshly ground chuck or a chuck and short rib blend
1 large yellow onion, 1 tablespoon very finely grated for sauce and the rest shaved paper-thin
1 tablespoon beef tallow or neutral oil, optional, divided
1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete
2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt or 1 1/4 teaspoons Morton kosher salt, for seasoning the patties
instructions: Grate 1 tablespoon from the yellow onion into a small bowl for the sauce, then shave the remaining onion paper-thin with a mandoline or sharp knife.
Spread the shaved onion on a paper towel-lined plate and pat very dry. Wet onion steams the beef instead of browning with it.

Make the burger sauce by stirring together 1/2 cup mayonnaise, 2 tablespoons ketchup, 1 tablespoon yellow mustard, 1 tablespoon pickle brine, the 1 tablespoon very finely grated onion and any onion juice, 1 teaspoon Worcestershire sauce, 1 teaspoon Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, 1/2 teaspoon smoked paprika, and 1/4 teaspoon garlic powder.
Cover and refrigerate the sauce for at least 15 minutes while preparing the burgers so the onion, pickle brine, and spices bloom into the mayonnaise.
Mix 2 teaspoons Diamond Crystal kosher salt, 1 teaspoon black pepper, and 1/2 teaspoon MSG if using in a small bowl. This is the patty seasoning.
Divide 1 1/2 pounds cold ground beef into 8 loose 3-ounce portions.
Gently shape each portion into a rough ball without packing the meat tightly.
Place the beef balls on a sheet pan and refrigerate for 15 minutes while preparing the buns and preheating the pan.
Spread the 2 tablespoons softened butter evenly over the cut sides of the 4 potato buns.

Preheat a cast-iron griddle or large cast-iron skillet over medium-high to high heat for 5 to 8 minutes, until a drop of water skitters and evaporates quickly or an infrared thermometer reads about 425 degrees F to 475 degrees F.
Toast the buttered buns cut-side down until deeply golden, then set them aside.
After the buns are toasted, return the pan to medium-high to high heat for 1 minute so it recovers before the beef goes in.

If the cooking surface is dry before the first batch, add 1 teaspoon beef tallow or neutral oil and spread it into a thin film with the spatula. Use up to the remaining 2 teaspoons only if the pan dries out between batches.
Work with 1 or 2 beef balls at a time so the pan stays hot and the patties have room.

Place each beef ball on the hot pan, immediately cover with a parchment square, and smash very hard with a burger press or heavy metal spatula for 8 to 10 seconds, until the patty is about 1/4 inch thick with ragged edges.
Remove the parchment and season the top of each patty with one-eighth of the salt, pepper, and MSG mixture, using all of the seasoning across the 8 patties.
Scatter one-eighth of the shaved onion over the raw top of each smashed patty, about 1/4 cup loosely packed onion per patty.
Cook without moving for 90 seconds to 2 minutes, until the edges are deeply browned and lacy.
Use a bench scraper or stiff spatula to scrape firmly under each patty so the crust stays attached to the beef.
Flip each patty onion-side down.
Immediately place 1 slice American cheese on each patty.
Cook for 45 to 75 seconds more, until the onions brown underneath, the cheese melts, and the beef is cooked through to 160 degrees F if checking with an instant-read thermometer.
Stack 2 patties together for each burger.
Spread 1 1/2 tablespoons burger sauce on each toasted bottom bun.
Add 1/2 cup shredded lettuce and 1/4 cup dill pickle chips to each bottom bun.
Place a double stack of smash patties on each prepared bottom bun.
Spread 1 tablespoon burger sauce on each toasted top bun and serve any remaining sauce on the side.
Close the burgers and serve immediately while the edges are still crisp.
notes: The big flavor moves are cold 80/20 beef, a ripping-hot pan, an immediate hard smash, dry shaved onion, American cheese, pickles, and a sauce with pickle brine and grated onion.
Do not mix salt into the ground beef before shaping. Salting the inside can make the patties tighter and more sausage-like.
Smash only once, right after the beef hits the pan. Pressing later squeezes out rendered fat and juices.
A loosely packed 3-ounce ball gives the best ratio of crispy crust to juicy middle for a double burger.
American cheese is intentional here because it melts fast enough to glue the two thin patties together.
Potato buns are ideal because they are soft, slightly sweet, and sturdy enough for sauce and beef fat.

Use a thin Louisiana-style cayenne hot sauce, such as Frank's RedHot, Crystal, or Texas Pete. Avoid smoky chipotle hot sauce, sweet hot sauce, fermented hot sauce, or extra-hot sauces here because they can overpower the burger sauce.
If the onions look wet after slicing, pat them dry again before cooking.
If the pan starts smoking aggressively or the onions burn before the cheese melts, lower the heat slightly for the next batch.
If the patties gray before they crust, the pan is too cool or too crowded.
Use a sharp-edged metal spatula or bench scraper. A flexible silicone spatula will not scrape the crust cleanly from the pan.
For a cleaner classic burger, skip the shaved onions and cook the patties plain, but the onion smash version has deeper diner-style flavor.
For USDA guidance, cook ground beef to 160 degrees F. These thin patties cook very quickly, so the main quality risk is overcooking after the crust has already formed.
Serve immediately. Smash burgers lose the magic as the lacy edges steam inside the bun.
title: Double Onion Smash Burgers
totalTime: 40 minutes plus 15 minutes beef chill time
